entry.title: Unable to change batch job spool output to Archive only
entry.content:
Storage mode cannot change to 'Archive only' when batch job scheduled through Solman job management. 

  1. Run SM37, and search for the given job
  2. Select the job with status 'Released' and go to 'Step'
  3. Display the step
  4. Click on 'Print Data' button at the bottom right
  5. In pop-up window, storage mode is not editable. Cannot change to other option, like 'Archive only'

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unable to change batch job spool output to Archive only
entry.content:
Hello,

To solve the issue, you may refer to the following SAP Community blog post: Print Parameter settings for Background jobs to avoid auto spool deletion

The issue in your case, is probably with the job status (covered in "Step 2" of the blog).

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Custom fields in S4 on-prem
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

There are several ways to create custom fields in SAP S4 on-prem :
- Old school way via SE11
- Using Eclispe ADT
- Using Fiori App "Custom Fields"

Two first ways leads to same results. 
Last one from Front-end app leads to generated objects in backend. 

What's the pro/cons of each ways to do that ? 

Thanks for your tips.
Regards

entry.title: SAP Data and Analytics Forum 12.-13.11.2024
entry.content:
We look forward to welcome you on the 12th to 13th. November at this year’s german speaking SAP Data and Analytics Forum. An exciting program with inspiring lectures and hands-on sessions awaits you.  

There are two ways to participate in the event:  

Option 1: You only visit the first day, in virtual format  

The first day is free and virtual. Current trends and developments in Data and Analytics are discussed here. From best practices to new technologies and applications such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere, you will be able to gain valuable insights and expand your knowledge.  

Anticipate three parallel tracks:  

  1. SAP Analytics Cloud: Strategy & Roadmap SAP Analytics Cloud  
  2. SAP Datasphere: Strategy & Roadmap SAP Datasphere 
  3. Extended Planning & Analytics: Potentials and insights from practical projects. 

Sign up here for the first free and virtual day (preliminary agenda)  

Option 2: You will visit both days on site in Walldorf  

If you also prefer personal communication, please visit us on the 12. and 13.11 at the SAP training center in Walldorf. The second paid day offers you the opportunity to work directly on the system and to deepen your skills and experiences in hands-on sessions. You can get to know new tools and functions and discuss them directly with our experts.  

You can expect three parallel tracks:  

  1. Hands-on Analytics: Experience SAP Analytics Cloud in different scenarios. Try out the use cases and exercises created specifically for the Forum directly in the system. Get ready for exciting exercises that delve into the interaction between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere, as well as address connectivity with SAP Analytics Cloud. 
  2. Hands-on Data Warehousing: This track focuses on SAP Datasphere. You can use scenarios created specifically for you to get to know the product yourself in the system. Practical exercises that cover delta handling of loading processes or SAP BW bridge integration await you. 
  3.  Hands-on Advanced: You already have experience with SAP Data and Analytics solutions and would like to dive deeper into the matter? Then visit the sessions of the Advanced Track. There are advanced exercises in SAP Analytics Cloud focusing on performance in story design or on best practices in SAP Datasphere security. Prepare yourself for a captivating exercise that provides insights into an AI use case with SAP HANA Cloud. 

You can choose flexibly between the sessions of the different tracks and thus put together their personal agenda.   

Don’t miss out on asking questions directly to our product management experts and our SAP trainers.   

Sign up here for the SAP Data and Analytics Forum right here. (Preliminary Agenda)

entry.title: Re: Old Instances Persisting in SAP Build Process Visibil...
entry.content: Hi, For the instances to not show up, the events should be deleted. Undeploy will take care of deleting the events but may not happen immediately as a scheduled job takes care of this in the background. What you can do to get rid of the older data is to go to Acquired Events application in Monitor tab. Apply the filter and search for all events with the process definition ID that is defined in the scenario model. Click on delete. Now in Manage Visibility scenarios, clear process data. With this , all data acquired so far will be removed. Hope this helps.

entry.title: Working with SAP Service Cloud Version 2, utilities-add-on: How to embed a Mashup
entry.content:
With Mashup being the way to extend SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add/on, I wanted to go through the steps needed for showing a Mashup in a new tab on the Agent Desktop. In addition, I will pass parameters to the Mashup that could be used for further logic.

I am doing this from a utility specific background, which is reflected in the type of data I am passing from Agent Desktop..

Disclaimer: This should not be considered a best practice for production, since this was done for a demo system.

 

When completed, the mashup looks like this when called from Agent Desktop:

The basic steps are:

  1. Create a Mashup (HTML page)
  2. Publish the Mashup (here I publish to GitHub)
  3. In SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add/on, whitelist the domain used for the mashup page.
  4. Configure mashup settings in SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add/on.
  5. Add Mashup in the Agent Desktop, using adaptation.

Create a Mashup (HTML page)

In order to embed the HTML page it is required that it allows to be embedded into an IFrame. I have created below HTML page, that is able to read parameters send to the page from SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add/on.

The data I plan to send to the Mashup is the Business Partner ID. In SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add/on the Business Partner can either be represented by the Account id (B2B Customer) or the Individual Customer id (Residential Customer). For this to work, we need to pass 2 parameters to the Mashup, one parameter for the Account and one parameter for the Residential Customer. In all scenarios either the account id or the individual customer id will be passed, but always only one of them.

In the above code I am just using JavaScript to read the parameters send from SAP Service Cloud Version 2.

 

Publish the Mashup

It is not possible to call the Mashup if it is stored on your local machine. I chose to use GitHub for publishing the page.

The steps to do this are simple:

  1. Log in to GitHub and create a new repository.
  2. Upload the HTML page.
  3. Go to your site's repository and set your publishing site.
  4. Once you've set and saved your publishing source, your website will be online.
  5. Copy the URL from the GitHub page, we will need this later when scoping the mashup in SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add/on.

I will not spend time documenting this in detail, there is a really good video of how to do this, please have a look here – LINK How to Host a Website On Github Pages (youtube.com)

 

In SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add-on, whitelist the domain used for the mashup page.

If this is the first time, we are adding a mashup for this domain, it is required to whitelist the domain.

In SAP Service Cloud Version 2 for utilities Add-on go to Setting:

Under ‘System Preferences’ select ‘Content Security Policy’.

This will open a list where the domain can be added to different sections.

It is possible to enter the domain for each section. I am not sure how the differentiation between these sections is made. Here is a list of the sections, and I have indicated where I added the domain ‘git.io’.

To create an entry – go to the section and click the ‘+’ sign.

 

Sections availableAdded domain
Script Sourcegit.io
Style Source 
Default Sourcegit.io
Frame-ancestors 
Frame-Sourcegit.io
Image Source 
Font Source ('font-src') 

NOTE: I am not sure if this a general behavior, but it seemed like I had a delay of something like 10 minutes between updating the list and when it was recognized by SAP Service Cloud Version 2.  Until it is recognized the message for trusted domain will persist in the configuration of the mashup.

 

Configure Mashup settings in SAP Service Cloud Version 2.

In this step we provide a name and decide how many parameters the mashup should be able to receive.

In Service Cloud Version 2 for Utilities Add-on go to Setting:

 

For ‘All Settings’, write mashup and then select ‘Mashup Authoring’.

 

Select the + sign for creating a new entry.

 

 

 

 

This will open the configuration view. Here we enter:

  1. Name of the mashup - this is the name we will see when looking for the mashup in adaptation.
  2. The URL - we have this from the step where we published the page to GitHub.
  3. Name of the first parameter - we are not making this mandatory, since we will either pass ‘par1’ or ‘par2’. Itis important to pass the same parameter names as we are reading in the HTML code.
  4. Name of the second parameter (same as for point 3).
  5. Here we have the option to see a preview of the mashup - it is not possible to see this pre-view, if the domain has not been whitelisted.

 

 

 

When we then save – the mashup will be provided an id by Service Cloud Version 2 for Utilities Add-on:

 

 

Add Mashup in the Agent Desktop, using adaptation.

The last step is to add the mashup to a new tab on the Agent Desktop.

Go to Agent Desktop and select ‘Start Adaptation’.

 

 

 

This will open Agent Desktop in adaptation mode.

First, we will add a new tab. Select the ‘+’ sign where we see the tabs:

 

 

Then go to the pencil on the right and then click the ‘+’ sign:

 

Enter the name of the tab.

NOTE: After entering the name, just click somewhere on the pop-up and it will be saved. For some reason the ‘Apply’ button stays grayed out.

 

 

After this the new tab is added to the view:

 

To add the mashup to this tab, click the ‘+’ sign under the tab row (MAKE SURE THAT THE NEWLY ADDED TAB IS SELECTED).

 

Select ‘>’ and then search for the name of the mashup.

 

 

 

Again select ‘>’ to get to the details of the mashup configuration.

 

 

On this view, we have the two parameters that was added as parameters. In the dropdown box, we can select the value we want to pass to each parameter.

The options of values to pass depends on the view we are adding the mashup into.

 

Parameter 'par1'Parameter 'par2'

After selecting ‘Apply’ the mashup will show on the view:

 

 

 

Then select ‘End Session’ at the top of the view and the configuration is completed.

 

 

Conclusion

I hope to have shown that adding a mashup, including passing parameters to the mashup, is quite simple.

Having the basics above done, it is now up to the implementer to decide what functionality that should be built into the mashup. The data passed can be used as the basis for this additional functionality.

entry.title: Electricity Flexibility: A must to succeed the grid transformation
entry.content:
​Electricity Grids Are Becoming the Largest Bottleneck of the Energy Transition

​​The energy transition is hunted by several challenges, including congestions & instability, where millions of EVs, solar panels, and heat pumps unfolding across the grid cause operators to confront unprecedented levels of over voltages, under voltages, overloads, and stability issues; Delays & Cancellations, as grids become the #1 cause of delays and cancellations of new renewable projects and electrification projects; and rising costs, where using the traditional approach alone could lead to a doubling of grid costs over the coming decade, causing consumer affordability issues.

​​Grid Management Approaches: Traditional Way vs. Using Flexibilities

​Managing electrical grids efficiently is crucial for ensuring a reliable supply of electricity and accommodating growing energy demands. Traditionally, grid management has relied heavily on extensive hardware and infrastructure investments. However, with advancements in technology and a better understanding of demand-side management, using flexibilities has emerged as a more efficient and cost-effective alternative.

​​The Traditional Way:

​Historically, grid management has focused on expanding supply-side infrastructure to meet peak demand and ensure grid stability. This approach involves:

  • More hardware: The traditional method emphasizes installing additional physical infrastructure, such as transformers, substations, and transmission lines, to handle increased loads.
  • Dimensioning for unmanaged peak loads: The grid is designed to manage the highest possible demand without actively regulating or smoothing out consumption peaks.
  • Multibillion € annual network upgrades: This approach requires significant financial investment for continuous expansion and upgrading of the grid to meet growing demands.
  • Years of construction works: Implementing these upgrades often involves long-term construction projects, which can disrupt communities and take years to complete.

​​With Flexibilities:

  • In contrast, leveraging demand-side flexibilities presents a modern and dynamic way to manage grid demands effectively. This method includes:
  • Software-driven solutions: Instead of relying solely on physical infrastructure, flexibilities use advanced software to monitor and manage energy consumption patterns in real-time.
  • Actively managing peak loads: By dynamically adjusting demand and using predictive analytics, this approach reduces the strain on the grid during peak times.
  • Cost-efficiency: Utilizing flexibilities is often faster and cheaper compared to extensive hardware upgrades. It minimizes the need for large-scale construction and significant capital expenditures.
  • Benefits for all stakeholders: This approach not only benefits grid operators but also provides advantages for customers and retailers by reducing energy costs and improving reliability

The table provides a comparative analysis of the challenges faced in managing Transmission System Operators (TSOs) and Distribution System Operators (DSOs) in electric grid operations. It highlights key aspects such as the number of nodes and topology, the number of power plants or virtual power plants (VPPs), computational complexity, communications and connectivity, and instrumentation. The purpose of the table is to illustrate the significant differences between TSOs and DSOs, emphasizing the higher complexity, larger scale, and unique challenges faced by DSOs in achieving flexible and intelligent grid operations. This comparison underscores the need for specialized solutions and technologies to address the distinct requirements of each type of operator.

Category

Transmission (TSOs)

Distribution (DSOs)

Number of nodes & Topology

Thousands

Tens of Millions

 

“Easy” to inspect and review manually by humans

Frequent errors in topology (mostly human-made), impossible to detect and inspect manually by humans

Number of Power Plants or VPPs

Thousands (mostly dispatchable)

Millions (Solar panels, EVs, Heat Pumps, Batteries, Aircons)

Computational complexity

x

xxxxxxxxxx

Communications and connectivity

Dedicated communication lines. Redundant

Wifi, Internet, PLC, consumer grade IOT... Frequent unavailability, bandwidth limitations, not redundant...

Instrumentation

High resolution, mission critical, control instrumentation. Redundant

Smart meters (designed for billing) Frequent unavailability of meters, and metering errors...

What are flexibilities and who is involved:

Flexibilities refer to the ability of the electricity system to adapt to changes in demand and supply. This adaptability is crucial for maintaining grid stability, integrating renewable energy sources, and optimizing energy use. Flexibilities can be provided by various actors within the energy ecosystem, each benefiting in different ways.

The concept of flexibilities in utilities encompasses the dynamic adjustments of demand and supply to maintain grid stability and integrate renewable energy. Various actors, including grid operators, energy suppliers, consumers, prosumers, aggregators, and technology providers, play crucial roles in this ecosystem. Each actor benefits uniquely, from cost savings and new revenue streams to enhanced grid reliability and sustainability.

How SAP is actively engaged?

​SAP is a participant in the BeFlexible (https://beflexible.eu/) project, a European initiative aimed at enhancing the flexibility of the electricity system. As part of a consortium of 21 partners from various sectors, SAP contributes its expertise in technology and data management to support the project's objectives. The BeFlexible project focuses on increasing the participation of prosumers in electricity generation, fostering cooperation between DSOs and TSOs, and developing new business models and regulatory frameworks. SAP’s engagement in the BeFlexible project is a natural progression from its contributions to previous EU Horizon 2020 initiatives like Flexiciency and Integrid.

​​SAP's role involves leveraging its solutions in data management and business technology to ensure data interoperability within the project's platforms and architectures. By providing utilities industry solutions and integration capabilities, SAP helps in designing and implementing the Grid Business and Data Network (GDBN), that allows operation and management of the energy system.

In the BeFlex project, SAP closely collaborate with INESC TEC, a renowned research and development institute based in Portugal. INESC TEC is dedicated to scientific and technological advancements in areas such as power systems, artificial intelligence, and robotics. Our partnership with INESC TEC enhances our ability to integrate innovative solutions into the Grid Data and Business Network (GDBN) platform.

​

SAP is actively involved in the pilot demonstrations of the BeFlexible project:

Congestion management (Madrid, Spain)

In this pilot, SAP is helping to manage congestion through demand response. The demonstration involves testing optimization algorithms for electric vehicle (EV) chargers and blockchain technology for energy trading platforms. Additionally, Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS) will be used to optimize energy consumption and costs for residential customers.

eMobility (France)

These pilots are located at SAP premises in Mougins and Caen. The focus is on eMobility, aiming to enhance the capabilities of charging point management systems to be active nodes in the flexible market. This includes optimizing for price and carbon footprint and providing value-added services to both end-users and site managers. The pilots also involve smart building technologies and supervised home charging points for SAP employees.

The illustration above shows a schematic workflow from flexibility capacitation through various service integrations to market operations and activation/settlement phases, emphasizing the iterative feedback loop from pilots to platform enhancement.

The GDBN supports the activities of the Flexibility-centric and Cross-sector Value Chain, namely Flex Capacitation and Integration/Enablement (by pairing Consumers/Prosumers with service providers), Aggregation and Activation & Settlement (by sending activation signals and making the settlement between Aggregators and DSO for the flexibility provided). It provides stakeholders (i.e., consumers, service providers, aggregators, DSOs) along the value chain of flexibility provision with an integrated ecosystem to support all main activities in this value chain, to help identifying, unlocking, and making use of all available flexibility potential.

The GBDN platform of the BeFlex project is illustrated in the provided diagram, highlighting the process of integrating various energy sources and flexibility providers across the value chain to enable flexible and intelligent grid operations. The diagram outlines the flow from the installation and retrofitting of assets (such as renewable energy sources, thermal loads, buildings, and homes) to flex capacitation involving FSPs, ESCos, ICT providers, and manufacturers. It then progresses to data integration services, followed by aggregation, market operation, and activation/settlement phases, incorporating pre-qualification, bid qualification, and flex activation. This structured approach ensures interoperability and effective market integration for energy services.

The Pre-qualification phase deals with the certification of Aggregators and flexible assets, ensuring that they are financially and technically able for participating in the flexibility market and to provide flexibility. This process may have different degrees of complexity, such as market and financial pre-qualification of the flexibility providers, or technical pre-qualification, which can refer to the product or to the grid constraints, which in turn can be conditional or dynamic. However, it is assumed that these processes are performed outside the GDBN, either in the market platform, by the DSO or both. The GDBN has the role of facilitating and increasing the interoperability of these processes

During negotiation DSO make their own grid analysis to assess potential constraints in the distribution grids. If one (or more) DSOs decide flexibility could solve some needs, the process to acquire flexibility using local market is started by publishing the flexibility need to which the Aggregators can respond by submitting flexibility bids if they have available resources in the DSO affected area.

In the Market Operation phase, the market clearing is performed inside the Market Platforms. Due to technical requirements, the DSO might depart from the bids selected by the Market Platforms and make the final bids selection based on their technical criteria.

In the Activation phase, reserved flexibility can be activated close to real time. Several approaches can be found in the literature, from those that only consider the activation of the flexibility previously reserved, to those that open a close to real-time market to see if new flexibility in the market can be more cost-effective than the flexibility previously reserved. Note that the flexibility selected for activation in the previous step is already integrated into the Aggregators baselines and do not need additional explicit activation.

In the last phase, Validation & Settlement, the flexibility delivered by each flexibility resource is calculated using their baselines and remuneration/penalties are settled between the DSO and the Aggregators and between each Aggregator and its resource owners. Since the GDBN concept is designed to provide services to multiple DSO, several alternatives can be considered.

​In the BeFlex project, several SAP solutions play crucial roles:

1.SAP Cloud for Energy: Facilitates the efficient management and a central place for energy consumption and distribution data.

2.SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP): Provides the technological foundation for integrating various services and technical platforms, ensuring seamless data interoperability.

3.SAP RISE: Supports digital transformation by offering flexible ERP deployment options used to invoice the participants.

4.SAP Measurement Concept Management: Model the infrastructure at customer site to enhance accuracy and reliability in energy distribution.

entry.title: The Transformative Power of ERP in Modern Businesses
entry.content:
Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems have become essential tools for businesses looking to streamline operations, enhance productivity, and gain a competitive edge in today's fast-paced market. These comprehensive software solutions integrate various business processes, such as finance, human resources, supply chain management, and customer relationship management, into a unified system. By doing so, ERP systems provide a holistic view of the business, enabling more informed decision-making and efficient resource utilization.

One notable provider of ERP solutions is Impactfirst, which offers a robust suite of ERP software tailored to meet the unique needs of different industries. Their platform stands out for its ability to simplify complex processes, enhance data accuracy, and improve operational efficiency. By consolidating disparate systems into a single platform, Impactfirst's ERP software helps businesses reduce redundancy, lower operational costs, and achieve better coordination across departments.

A key advantage of implementing an ERP system like the one from Impactfirst is the improved visibility it offers into business operations. With real-time data and analytics, managers can monitor performance, identify trends, and address issues promptly. This level of insight is crucial for strategic planning and can significantly impact a company's ability to adapt to market changes and capitalize on new opportunities.

Moreover, Impactfirst's ERP solutions are designed with scalability in mind, ensuring that businesses can continue to benefit from the software as they grow and their needs evolve. Whether a company is looking to improve inventory management, enhance customer service, or optimize financial reporting, Impactfirst provides the tools necessary to achieve these goals efficiently.

In conclusion, ERP systems are vital for modern businesses seeking to optimize their operations and maintain a competitive edge. Providers like Impactfirst are leading the way by offering comprehensive, adaptable, and user-friendly ERP solutions that address the diverse needs of today's enterprises. By investing in a robust ERP system, businesses can ensure they are well-equipped to navigate the complexities of the modern market and drive long-term success.

For more information on how Impactfirst's ERP software can transform your business, visit Impactfirst ERP.

entry.title: Re: Monitor a specific Database Table for no. of entries
entry.content:
Hi,

Please be aware of that there is no standard metric in SAP standard template.

For all the available metrics can be checked in the following path:

SOLMAN_SETUP > Application Operations > Step 4. Template Maintenance > Database category> select one template name > Metrics tab

If expected metric not included in standard template, please consider to refer to the following KBA to create your own custom metrics instead:

2484872 - Requested metric not included in standard templates

Or, please consider using the SAP Influence Program where new ideas for non-existent functionalities can be noted so that they later can be evaluated and, if they result useful and upvoted, DEV can include them in future releases or patches. More info can be found here:
11 - Would you like to shape & influence future releases of SAP Products? - SAP for Me

Best Regards,
Weihua

entry.title: Error en Query - Facturas pagas en plazo (60 días)
entry.content:
Hola expertos!! Estoy haciendo una query que me muestre una lista de facturas que se han pagado en un plazo determinado. Hay algunas lineas de la query que me salen repetidas y no entiendo por que

Intuyo que es algo en la vinculacion con los pagos pero en las lineas repetidas salen pagos que no son ni del mismo cliente de la factura. 

Se os ocurre que puedo cambiar o que he puesto mal?

Gracias!!!

SELECT DISTINCT
    T2."DocNum",
    T2."DocEntry" AS "Navegar a factura",
    T4."SeriesName",
    T2."DocStatus",
    T2."TaxDate" as "Fecha de documento",
    T2."CardCode",
    T2."CardName",
    (T2."DocTotal" - T2."VatSum") AS "Total Sin IVA",
    T2."DocTotal",
    T2."PaidToDate",
    T9."DocNum" AS "Numero de Documento de Pago",
    T9."DocDate" AS "Fecha de contabilización de Pago",
    CASE 
        WHEN T9."DocDate" IS NOT NULL THEN ''
        ELSE T7."LineMemo"
    END AS "Comentario Linea Asiento",
    CASE 
        WHEN T9."DocDate" IS NOT NULL THEN ''
        ELSE T11."RefDate"
    END AS "Fecha Pago Asiento",
    T6."BPLName",
    CASE
        WHEN DAYS_BETWEEN(T2."TaxDate", T9."DocDate") IS NOT NULL THEN DAYS_BETWEEN(T2."TaxDate", T9."DocDate")
        ELSE DAYS_BETWEEN(T2."TaxDate", T11."RefDate")
        END AS "DiasPago",
    CASE
         WHEN CASE                 
                  WHEN T9."DocDate" IS NOT NULL THEN DAYS_BETWEEN(T2."TaxDate", T9."DocDate")
                 ELSE DAYS_BETWEEN(T2."TaxDate", T11."RefDate")
                 END < 60 THEN 'OK'
        ELSE 'NO'
    END AS "MENOS60"

FROM OPDN T0 
LEFT JOIN PDN1 T1 ON T0."DocEntry" = T1."DocEntry"  
RIGHT JOIN OPCH T2 ON T1."BaseEntry" = T2."DocEntry" 
LEFT JOIN PCH1 T3 ON T2."DocEntry" = T3."DocEntry"  
INNER JOIN NNM1 T4 ON T2."Series" = T4."Series" 
INNER JOIN OSLP T5 ON T2."SlpCode" = T5."SlpCode" 
INNER JOIN OBPL T6 ON T2."BPLId" = T6."BPLId" 
LEFT JOIN JDT1 T7 ON T7."TransId" = T2."TransId" 
LEFT JOIN VPM2 T8 ON T8."DocEntry" = T2."DocEntry"  
LEFT JOIN OVPM T9 ON (T9."DocEntry" = T8."DocNum")
LEFT JOIN OACT T10 ON T7."Account" = T10."AcctCode" 
LEFT JOIN OJDT T11 ON T7."TransId" = T11."TransId" 

WHERE T2."CANCELED" = 'N'
  AND T6."BPLName" Like '%%[%0]%%'
  AND YEAR(T2."TaxDate") = 2023

ORDER BY
T2."DocNum"

entry.title: From Idea to Market: Accelerating MVP Development with Rapid Prototyping
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business landscape, getting your product to market quickly and efficiently can mean the difference between success and failure. This is where the concept of rapid prototyping shines, especially in the realm of Minimum Viable Product (MVP) development.

In this article, we'll delve into how rapid prototyping can empower the MVP Development Company to transform ideas into market-ready MVPs quickly and efficiently.

Understanding Rapid Prototyping

Rapid prototyping is a development approach that emphasizes building quick, scaled-down versions of a product to test its functionality, design, and market viability. Unlike traditional methods that involve extensive planning upfront, rapid prototyping focuses on getting a tangible product in front of users as soon as possible.

The Rapid Prototyping Process

Rapid prototyping is a product development method to swiftly create an initial version of a product or its part for validating the design. Here's an overview of the rapid prototyping process:

  • Idea Generation: The process starts with brainstorming and idea generation sessions to outline the basic concept of the product.
  • Conceptualization: Once the idea is formed, it's time to create rough sketches, wireframes, or simple mockups to visualize the product.
  • Prototyping: This stage involves building a basic prototype of the product. It can range from low-fidelity paper prototypes to high-fidelity interactive prototypes.
  • Testing and Feedback: The prototype is then tested with real users to gather feedback on its usability, features, and overall concept.
  • Iteration: Based on the feedback received, iterations are made to the prototype to improve its functionality and user experience.

Advantages of Rapid Prototyping in MVP Development

Rapid prototyping plays a crucial role in MVP (Minimum Viable Product) development by allowing teams to quickly validate ideas, gather feedback, and iterate on product concepts. Here are some advantages of using rapid prototyping in MVP development:

  • Speed: Rapid prototyping significantly reduces the time it takes to go from an idea to a functional prototype, enabling faster market entry.
  • Cost-effectiveness: By identifying and addressing issues early in the development process, rapid prototyping saves both time and resources.
  • User-Centric Design: Early user feedback allows for the development of products that better meet user needs and preferences.
  • Flexibility: Rapid prototyping allows for quick iterations and changes, ensuring the final product is aligned with market demands.

Successfully MVPs Developed with Rapid Prototyping

Rapid prototyping is a fantastic approach for developing MVPs (Minimum Viable Products) quickly and efficiently. Here are several successful MVPs developed using rapid prototyping:

  1. Snapchat: Snapchat amously started as a simple prototype called Picaboo. Through rapid prototyping and user feedback, it evolved into the popular social media platform we know today.
  2. Dropbox: Dropbox created a basic video demo to showcase their idea before writing a single line of code. This approach helped them validate the concept and attract early adopters.
  3. Zappos: Zappos initially tested the concept of online shoe retailing with a simple website and photos taken at local shoe stores. This rapid prototyping approach validated their business idea before investing heavily in inventory.

Tools and Techniques for Rapid Prototyping

Rapid prototyping is crucial for quickly validating ideas, iterating on designs, and getting early feedback from users before investing significant time and resources. Here are various tools and techniques for rapid prototyping:

  • Wireframing Tools: Tools like Balsamiq, Sketch, or Figma are popular for creating low-fidelity wireframes.
  • Prototyping Platforms: Platforms such as InVision, Adobe XD, or Proto.io allow for creating interactive prototypes.
  • MVP Frameworks: Using frameworks like Lean Startup or Design Sprint can streamline the rapid prototyping process.

Conclusion

Rapid prototyping is a powerful methodology for accelerating MVP development, allowing companies to iterate quickly, reduce time-to-market, and build products that resonate with users. By embracing rapid prototyping, startups and businesses can increase their chances of success in today's competitive market.

entry.title: SAP Data Services: Behavior confirmation for SQL query submitted for reader in 4.2 vs 4.3
entry.content:
Hello Team,

We are in the upgrade process of SAP Data Services from version 4.2 to version 4.3.
We are observing a behavior change in the way SQL query is submitted for the reader in v4.3 and v4.2.

We have a job that will fetch data from 3 tables. When we checked the Query Transform object then we found that one of the tables is neither used in the select clause nor in the where clause.

For ex:
Let us assume we have 3 tables namely A, B and, C. In the data flow, the C table is neither used in the select clause nor the where clause. 

We ran the ETL on v4.2 and v4.3 and below is the observation from the logs:

  1. V4.2 logs: SQL query for the table that is not used is not submitted for the reader
  2. V4.3 logs: SQL query for the table that is not used is submitted for the reader

Question:
Is this an expected behavior of SAP Data Services for the respective version?

Note:
We understand that if the table is not used in the data flow then we should remove it but before doing that we want to understand this is the expected behavior or not.

entry.title: Differences between SAP ECC and S/4 HANA in Customer Service Module
entry.content:
Hello experts.

I am a novice to SAP and I want to know several things about SAP ECC and S/4 HANA in Customer Service Module.

About Customer Service Module

With the change from ECC to S4HANA, the Customer Service Module is now more tightly coupled with S/4 HANA itself, rather than with the SFA.

  • What changes have been made to the ERP function, the Customer Service Module function, and the SFA function as a result of this change?
  • How has the content of the Customer Service Module itself changed?
  • What changes have been made to the internal logic, system behavior, etc., and what are the effects of these changes?
  • What are some of the things in the Customer Service Module that were not possible in the ECC, but can now be done with S/4 HANA?
  • On the other hand, is there any thing in the Customer Service Module that was possible in the ECC but is no longer possible in S/4 HANA?

 

Please reply if you know the answer to any of these questions. Thanks for your help.

Best regards,

Reo

 

SAP S/4HANA

entry.title: Re: How do we confirm yield and scrap manually in workcen...
entry.content:
Hi, for Work Center and Operation Activity PODs, yield confirmation is posted automatically when you complete each operation, if the operation is marked as a SAP ERP reporting step. See: Production Yield Confirmation on Operation Complete.

Scrap confirmation on the other hand, is posted when you scrap a SFC, logging a Nonconformance for example. See: Scrap Confirmation for a Production Order.

Hope it helps,
Manoel

entry.title: The intersection of AI and SAP BASIS
entry.content:
I agree with the perspective that technological innovation, including AI, is a tool that can lead us to success if we utilize it effectively. It’s not about replacing us but enhancing our capabilities and helping us visualize and solve problems in ways we couldn’t before.

Regarding SAP BASIS, while it’s true that the landscape is changing with the advent of cloud platforms and initiatives like RISE with SAP, the core understanding of how SAP works that BASIS professionals bring is invaluable. BASIS is more than just installation, upgradation, or maintenance. It’s about understanding the client’s business needs and providing technical solutions to meet those needs.

The key, as with any profession, is to adapt and evolve. Learning new concepts, like Agile methodologies, and gaining a deeper understanding of business problem statements will indeed be crucial for BASIS professionals to stay relevant in this changing landscape.

I look forward to hearing more views on this topic from the community.

Best Regards,
Rishi Raj Singh

entry.title: Re: DFKKRP-XACCEPTCHARGES activation by default, SAP FICA...
entry.content:
For new (automatically) created lots you can adjust the flags during upload of the files with event 964 (also see 960/ 963) to set the flag DFKKRK-XACCEPTCHARGE. 

If you create the return lots manually, please try to use a transaction variant (via SHD0) and set the flag DFKKRK-XACCEPTCHARGE.

entry.title: Re: Old Instances Persisting in SAP Build Process Visibil...
entry.content: Thanks for resolving the issue together with me. For anyone else with the same problem: you need to filter with the whole process definition id (including all prefixes and region) to be able to delete the events. The filter will work with only the process name, but you will not be able to delete the events. This fixed the issue for me, thanks for the help.

entry.title: Unleashing the power of SAP Companion
entry.content:
About in-app help

In-app help is more than just field-level help. We strive for the most comprehensive in-app help experience to help you perform your tasks within the app itself without having to constantly jump back and forth between the app and help resources. In addition to field-level help, the What’s This App? tile, and in-app What’s New, some product areas provide additional content and features based on the customer feedback they’ve received, such as:

  • How-to videos, how-to procedures, FAQs, and configuration information in Finance
  • How-to videos, how-to procedures, Welcome tiles, and video flows in Sourcing and Procurement

How do you find the in-app help in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud system?

First, how do you access the in-app help? On the SAP Fiori Launchpad, the question mark tells you that SAP Companion - our native in-app help tool - is active but not open. Click the question mark to display the Help Panel and display the SAP Fiori Launchpad in-app help. You will see generic information and instructions on how to navigate in the system.

Let’s for example open the Manage Processing Rules – For Bank Statements app. Click the question mark to display the in-app help:

What are the Help Panel, the Search, and the Quick Tour?

The Help Panel on the right displays both generic and app-specific content, the Search field helps you find in-app help on a particular app screen, and the Quick Tour is a generic introduction to the app UI that you can go through sequentially:

 

What’s the “What’s This App?” tile?

The What’s This App? tile links to the app description in the product assistance on SAP Help Portal, where you can find more information about the app and business information as well:

What are hotspots?

Hotspots are question mark icons on the app UI that tell you that field-level help is available for a specific field. Hover over them to display the content. You can also access the content from the Help Panel. Field-level help ranges from simple text, as in the first example, to more comprehensive help with tables and graphics, as in the second example:

What’s the Learning tile?

You may have noticed the Learning icon screenshot next to the Help Panel:

Click the icon to open the Learning tile. The Learning Center tile contains Learning assets. Learning assets can be tutorials or interactive demos that help you familiarize yourself with an app and learn how to navigate within it.

ℹ️ Not all apps offer Learning assets.

The following example is drawn from the Manage Automatic Payments app in Finance:

What’s the Community Tile?

The SAP S/4HANA Cloud community contains a wealth of resources and is the perfect place for interaction. You can search for information, ask questions, and engage with SAP experts, customers, and partners.

What are FAQs?

Frequently asked questions are compilations of questions and answers that are part of the product assistance or an SAP Note and that enlighten you on specific aspects of a functionality:

What are how-to procedures and how-to videos?

How-to procedures are step-by-step procedures, whereas how-to videos are the visual equivalents of such procedures. In the following examples, how-to procedures and how-to videos are part of the same tile:

ℹ️You can watch the videos full screen by pressing the corresponding button (). Most videos are provided in English only but with multilingual captions.

What’s a Welcome tile?

A Welcome tile is a custom tile that redirects you to a wide range of useful resources, so that you become familiar with a specific process, for example, the Source-to-Pay process in Sourcing and Procurement:

What deliverables are available with configuration apps?

Configuration apps allow configuration experts in Finance to check and adapt configuration settings. They are shipped with a wide range of deliverables. Let’s have a closer look at, for example, the Renumber G/L Accounts configuration app:

You may wonder why there are so many resources. This is simply because configuration requires information from different channels. The more information we provide to the implementer, the better.

Is further information available in field-level help?

In-app help can have links to further information on SAP Help Portal. This is the case when the field-level help doesn’t suffice or when more contextual information is needed.

Alternatively, you can find further information on SAP Help Portal in the Help Panel:

What’s in-app What’s New?

In-app help What’s New informs you about new, changed, renamed, or deprecated apps, as well as new or changed features in apps in an SAP S/4HANA Cloud release.

When you open an app, a banner informs you about the changes:

Please note the color used to draw your attention (orange). If you click it away, you won’t see the banner next time you open your app. To call it up again, click the  icon.

Here’s an example of a new app in Finance:

Here’s an example of a changed feature in Finance:

Here’s another example in Sales:

And another one in Sourcing and Procurement:

What are guided tours?

Guided tours consist of a sequence of interactive steps that help you perform a specific task in an app.

Click the  icon to open the guided tour.

Here’s an example from Finance:

And another one from Sales:

Can you enhance our in-app help or create your own one?

If you want to enhance our in-app help or create your own one, you can do this with SAP Enable Now. For more information, see the blog post below.

To summarize

We hope that we've stimulated your curiosity and that you'll make the most of our in-app help offering. Just remember that behind the little question mark, there's a wealth of resources to help you get your job done, efficiently and reliably. Your feedback is of course highly appreciated. Just add a comment to this blog. 😊

Stay tuned for more updates, as SAP Companion is constantly shipping exciting features to improve the product experience!

More information

If you want to know more about SAP Companion, SAP Enable Now, and how to configure SAP Companion, please check this blog:

Demystifying Common Questions: A Quick Dive into SAP Companion, SAP Enable Now and More

entry.title: Artificial Intelligence in the Work of SAP Basis Consultants
entry.content:
The advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) is transforming numerous areas of business, and the realm of enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems is no exception. In particular, SAP Basis consultants, who are responsible for the technical administration of the SAP environment, are witnessing how AI can significantly change and improve their daily work. In this blog, we will explore how AI is revolutionizing the tasks of SAP Basis consultants, increasing efficiency, reducing errors, and enabling a more strategic focus.

1. Automation of Repetitive Tasks and Predictive Maintenance

One of the main advantages of AI is its ability to automate repetitive and maintenance tasks. SAP Basis consultants can greatly benefit from this automation, freeing up their time to focus on higher-value activities.

Example: AI can automate the monitoring of SAP systems, detecting and correcting common issues such as configuration errors, performance failures, or database inconsistencies before they impact users. Additionally, through predictive maintenance, AI can foresee when hardware or software failures are likely to occur, allowing consultants to schedule interventions before disruptions happen.

2. System Performance Optimization

AI can analyze large volumes of system performance data in real-time to identify patterns and trends that human consultants might overlook. This enables continuous and proactive optimization of the SAP system’s performance.

Example: Using machine learning algorithms, AI can identify peak load times in the system and automatically adjust resources to balance the workload, ensuring SAP systems run optimally at all times. This is especially useful in environments with significant demand fluctuations, such as during financial closings or major sales events.

3. Enhanced Security and Compliance

Security is a constant concern for SAP Basis consultants. AI can provide an additional layer of protection by continuously monitoring for suspicious activities and identifying potential threats.

Example: AI systems can analyze access and usage patterns to detect anomalous behaviors that might indicate a security breach attempt. If suspicious activity is detected, the system can alert consultants or even take preventive measures automatically, such as blocking accounts or restricting access to certain resources until a review is conducted.

4. Assistance in Problem Resolution

Resolving technical issues in SAP environments can be complex and time-consuming. AI can act as an intelligent assistant, helping diagnose problems and providing solutions based on historical data and failure patterns.

Example: AI-powered virtual assistants can help SAP Basis consultants troubleshoot technical issues by offering real-time suggestions. For instance, if a performance problem is detected, the virtual assistant could suggest specific adjustments to the system configuration or the application of certain patches based on previous solutions to similar problems.

5. Continuous Training and Development

AI can also play a significant role in the training and continuous development of SAP Basis consultants. AI-driven learning platforms can customize training programs based on individual needs and the experience level of each consultant.

Example: An intelligent learning system could recommend specific courses, tutorials, or training modules based on the areas where a consultant needs improvement or the latest updates and new functionalities of the SAP system. This ensures that consultants are always up-to-date with best practices and emerging technologies.

Conclusion

Artificial intelligence is changing how SAP Basis consultants perform their work, providing powerful tools to automate routine tasks, improve system performance, ensure security, facilitate problem resolution, and support continuous professional development. By adopting these technologies, consultants can significantly increase their efficiency and effectiveness, allowing them to focus on strategic tasks that add greater value to their organizations.

In a world where technology and business evolve rapidly, integrating AI into the SAP Basis field is not just a competitive advantage but a necessity to stay ahead and ensure optimal and secure operation of enterprise systems.

Note: This article was created with ChatGPT AI.

Best Regards,

Eli Daniel Ramones

entry.title: Re: Initialize delta flag for a Delta DTP in BW Bridge
entry.content:
Hello @SulaimanS,

If all requests created by the delta DTP are deleted from the target, the next request will be automatically an init.
If you need to Re-Init a delta DTP you can drop the content of the target and then run the DTP again.
This will get automatically a new delta

BR.

entry.title: What happened to the uploaded cards in Work Zone and Why?
entry.content:
In Q2 2024, SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition released the support of role-based access to UI Integration Cards. While bringing increased flexibility of workpages (by allowing cards to be automatically filtered according to user's roles), that also is a significant step forward into a unified content model of cards, apps, and roles. Card is no longer a standalone entity, but is now always a visualization of an App (short for "Business Application"), which in turn can be assigned to Roles for access control and dynamic filtering features. However, with those shifts on concepts and features, previous ways of card management has been changed as well.

Change #1: Upload Card button has disappeared from Admin Console

Upload Card button is removed

By disallowing a card to be uploaded directly and used standalone, we now make sure there is always an app which the cards belong to. And with role assignments to the app, the card can then have role-based access control.

Change #2: Card creation/deletion is managed by App from the Content Manager

Because of that change, all the existing cards which were uploaded directly to Work Zone will now be migrated to become visualizations of some apps. That's why you will see a list of new local apps showing up in the Content Manager. They have the same title as their card visualization.

local apps created out of the cards

And if you want to delete a particular card, you can no longer do so on the Admin Console UI, but must delete its app on the Content Manager UI.

Delete option is gone

Here's the step-by-step guide illustrating the complete flow of the new way of card management: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/new-card-upload-process-and-role-based-filtering-of-cards-in-work-zone-adv/ba-p/13690288. Of course, that might appear to be a lot of unnecessary effort in addition, if all you want is to deploy a card and use it as before. But what comes along is the additional capability on access control for cards based on the roles. This additional layer between roles and cards also makes it possible to decouple the permission definitions with the actual controlled entities. That is, an app could at any time be updated to host a new card as its visualization, with all role-assignments, navigation target, and app-level settings unchanged; a card could also point to a dynamic navigation target controlled by its app. In the future, it will even be possible for one app to have multiple visualizations, e.g. several different tiles and cards belonging to one app. That would be a common case for several cards inside the same content package, where they all link to the same target system (only differ in relative paths or URL params) and are supposed to be exposed to the same group of users, thus no need to repeat the configurations for each card.

What happened exactly during the card migration?

A migration will be run for all existing Work Zone customers, during which a local app will be automatically created for each card, configured to take the card as its visualization. And the wrapping app's id is always set to "<card_id>.app", with title and description set to the same with the card. Once migrated, the wrapping app will be assigned the Everyone role, so that the card is still exposed to all end users as today (of course, the restrictions defined in Card Access List will continue to work on top). Later, if customer wants, the admin could change the role assignment of the app to restrict the card's access to a smaller group of users who have the corresponding roles.

What can go wrong and what to do in case of failures?

However, there are a few cases, although rare, that might result in failure during the creation of the wrapping app or the association between the card and its wrapping app. The most likely causes are:

  • Card id is longer than 166 characters (because there's a limitation of 170 for App id)
  • Special characters (such as "-->") in Title or Description

When such unfortunate situation does happen, the wrapping app will not be created. But don't worry, the failed cards will continue to work as before. The delete option will still be there for such cards. And you can tell whether there are any failures by checking if the delete option still exists for any cards or by checking if any cards don't have a corresponding app (with the same title) listed in the content manager.

Delete option is still there for failed cards

You can delete such a card directly from the Admin Console UI, and then try checking and fixing the problems and creating a new local app with the card uploaded as visualization. If you still encounter failure and can't figure out the root cause, please do not hesitate to contact SAP support by filing an incident ticket.

entry.title: Handling Unpacked Dangerous Goods in Intra and Intercompany STOs in S/4 HANA Product Compliance
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I need some guidance on handling unpacked dangerous goods in purchasing scenarios, specifically for intra-company stock transfer orders (STOs) and intercompany STOs, using the S/4 HANA Product Compliance application on premises.

Context:

  • Dangerous goods checks can only be processed successfully for products with a released dangerous goods assessment for packaged products.
  • The item on the purchase order must be a packaged product.

Question: How should we handle the transfer of unpacked dangerous goods between plants within the same company or between different companies? Are there any best practices or configurations to ensure compliance and safety during such transfers?

Any insights or experiences would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

Best regards,

Ajit Dude
